content:
We have ARef::from_raw(), which doesn't increase the refcount itself, i.e. it takes away the reference count of the input pointer. However, Device::from_raw() will increase the refcount of device, i.e. it uses its own reference count.

I find this may be a bit confusing in the future, hence bring this up. Thoughts? @Danilo Krummrich
I would say that for from_raw, it shouldn't increment the refcount for consistency with te standard library Arc::from_raw.

I agree with Alice, but for a different reason. I'd argue that ARef::from_raw takes ownership of the reference that is passed in. In Device we need an extra increment of the refcount, because we don't own the reference of the raw device pointer that is passed to us, but instead are only guaranteed that it's valid for the duration of the function to increase the reference count to take our own one.

Yes, so a -> ARef<_> function may have at least two semantics: 1) using the reference count provided by the caller, or 2) using an own reference count. I feel we better have some general naming rule to separate these two cases.
For example, case #2 could have a get_ prefix indicating the refcount increased.

Danilo Krummrich said:

I agree with Alice, but for a different reason. I'd argue that ARef::from_raw takes ownership of the reference that is passed in. In Device we need an extra increment of the refcount, because we don't own the reference of the raw device pointer that is passed to us, but instead are only guaranteed that it's valid for the duration of the function to increase the reference count to take our own one.

I am a bit confused, you said that you agree with Alice, but you say that you want from_raw to increment the refcount?
another alternative for your current situation would be to use let r = Device::as_ref(..); and then ARef::from(r)
then we could avoid having a from_raw function.

Benno Lossin said:

Danilo Krummrich said:

I agree with Alice, but for a different reason. I'd argue that ARef::from_raw takes ownership of the reference that is passed in. In Device we need an extra increment of the refcount, because we don't own the reference of the raw device pointer that is passed to us, but instead are only guaranteed that it's valid for the duration of the function to increase the reference count to take our own one.

I am a bit confused, you said that you agree with Alice, but you say that you want from_raw to increment the refcount?

AFAICS, I did not. I said that in Device::from_raw we need to increment the ref-count by one, because we don't own a reference to the Device yet. ARef::from_raw expects us to own the reference to the thing we pass the ARef::from_raw though.
I understood Alice in that all from_raw functions should take control of a refcount
Oh, you mean as in every method named from_raw?
that's how I understood it, yes, but I don't know if Alice also meant it that way

I understood it as the semantics of ARef::from_raw should be aligned to upstream Arc::from_raw.

I don't think that Device::from_raw and ARef::from_raw should have the same semantics, actually for Device::from_raw that'd be a bug.
Device::from_raw doesn't consume the reference, that is passed in.
I’d suggest we change the name of Device::from_raw() instead of the implementation

Benno Lossin said:

another alternative for your current situation would be to use let r = Device::as_ref(..); and then ARef::from(r)

can't we just do this and avoid the from_raw function alltogether?
We can, but having a function doing the above doesn’t hurt, right? Plus if users have to repeat this again and again, they would introduce their version of a helper function.
but to some degree, you are right, the implementation of that function (whose name now is Device::from_raw()) should use as_ref() + from(), instead of directly calling get_device()

e.g.

impl Device {
    /// Gets a refcounted reference to [`Self`]
    ///
    /// # Safety
    /// `ptr` must be a valid pointer with at least one reference count until the function return.
    pub unsafe fn get_from_raw(ptr: *mut device) -> ARef<Self> {
        // SAFETY: Per function safety requirement, `ptr` is a valid pointer and has at least one
        // reference count until the function return, which is larger then the scope of the `&Device`
        // created by `Device::as_ref(ptr)`.
        unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into()
    }
}
Sounds reasonable, I'd call it from_raw_get though.

I wouldn't create such a function in the first place. How many times do you need to call it anyways? I think writing

let dev = unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into();

instead of

let dev = unsafe { Device::from_raw_get(ptr) };

doesnt really hurt. and creating the additional function increases the total number of unsafe by 2
I should say I'm OK with removing the function if no real user, I was more worried about the inconsistency.
but we certainly need more document on the usage of the ".into()" above.

Generally, I'm fine with just doing

let dev = unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into();

but I think we need to consider new people looking at the API of Device only finding Device::as_ref.

Having a dedicated Device::from_raw_get makes it obvious to new people that this is an option.
we could add this as an example on the docs on debice and/or on as_ref
Would you like to submit a patch doing that or guide someone else (via a first good issue maybe)? :)

@Benno Lossin Sounds reasonable, adding an example might be tricky though, since we can't create a valid Device in the example. I made the Firmware example no_run for that purpose and did hide the Device creation, since it's not relevant for the Firmware example.

@Boqun Feng Do you mean Benno or me?
I meant Benno
I currently don't have the bandwidth, but if you want to create a good-first-issue, then go ahead

Boqun Feng said:

I meant Benno

Because like I said, I would just rename the Device::from_raw() into Device::from_raw_get(), and I I take it Danilo you are OK with it. But if Benno thinks it's important to avoid Device::from_raw_get() at all, I'm happy to see a patch from him ;-)

Benno Lossin said:

I currently don't have the bandwidth, but if you want to create a good-first-issue, then go ahead

Done: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1088

@Danilo Krummrich @Benno Lossin let me know if I should change anything ;-)
By the way, I copied and pasted the "This requires ..." paragraph from other first good issues, it's very handy ;-)

looks good!

IIUC the device abstractions aren't upstreamed yet, right? in that case it might be easier for Danilo to just change the next version
It's already in Greg's tree
and it's already rc7, so likely we cannot make it.
but if Danilo has his own plan, I'm happy to close the issue.
did Greg say that he picked the patches? I don't see such a mail

I don't think he mentioned this at the exact time he picked it up (he said he was going to at v3, and he picked at v4), it's now sitting in device-core tree:

https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/driver-core.git/commit/?h=driver-core-next&id=a674fefd17324fc467f043568e738b80ca22f2b4
When Greg applies something to his tree, only the submitter (and maybe tags) get notified by a bot.
Yeah, I would just call it something else than from_raw. E.g., you can call it get_device to reuse the name of the existing C function of the same name.

Hello! I have tried to solve this issue as my first contribution and im having a little trouble with replacing

let dev = unsafe { Device::from_raw_get(ptr) };

with

let dev = unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into();

The Device struct does not implement the necessary traits for the as_ref() call. From what I understand this is to be submitted in this patch:
https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/20240618234025.15036-10-dakr@redhat.com/.
How should i proceed?

@Panagiotis Foliadis Device implements a method as_ref.

In rust/kernel/pci.c you can call it like this:

let dev: ARef<device::Device> = unsafe { device::Device::as_ref(&mut (*pdev).dev) }.into();

If we go for removing Device::from_raw entirely, then a patch would only need to do exactly this, remove the function.

If we go for a better implementation (which I personally still prefer), namely Device::from_raw_get, you do something similar to the above in that method.

Danilo Krummrich said:

Panagiotis Foliadis Device implements a method as_ref.

Does Device::as_ref exist on any of the rust-for-linux upstream branches?

Ah, this is on it's way upstream (Linus' tree) and sits in Greg's tree until it's merged in the next days.

Use this tree as base: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/driver-core.git/log/?h=driver-core-next

Danilo Krummrich said:

Ah, this is on it's way upstream (Linus' tree) and sits in Greg's tree until it's merged in the next days.

Use this tree as base: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/driver-core.git/log/?h=driver-core-next

Oh woah some of your work got upstream? Nice :D
Yeah, baby steps we take. :)

Ok so im having a couple of issues in understanding what this issue needs exactly.

From what I understand, the Device::from_raw function returns a mutable reference to a Device(Opaque<bindings::phy_device) struct by passing a bindings::phy_device mutable pointer.
The as_ref function (which needs the implementation of the AsRef trait in the Device struct) is a reference to reference conversion, therefore it has a different functionality.

Also the as_ref method does not exist in the https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/gregkh/driver-core.git/log/?h=driver-core-next branch for the Device(Opaque<bindings>::phy_device) struct and im still not sure if i need to implement this or just remove the from_raw function (since just removing the from_raw function will not compile).

You're looking at phy::Device. We're talking about the generic device::Device though.

The code also just hit Linus' tree, you can find device::Device::from_raw here: https://git.kernel.org/pub/scm/linux/kernel/git/torvalds/linux.git/tree/rust/kernel/device.rs#n54

Sorry for the late response!
I have a question about the examples that I need to add to the documentation.
For the case of the Device::as_ref I believe the 

let dev = unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into();

is enough, however im struggling to find a correct example to add to the ARef struct that connects to the removal of the Device::from_raw function (since the github issues specifies that an example should be added to the ARef struct).
The Device::as_ref + into() doesnt have anything to do with the ARef struct right?

Boqun Feng said:

We can, but having a function doing the above doesn’t hurt, right? Plus if users have to repeat this again and again, they would introduce their version of a helper function.

I degree with @Boqun Feng when he say that if users have to repeat this again, again and again ... Some people will create the help function.
I think that we should rename this function for get_from_raw() for difference between Arch::from_raw() and inside this function only call Device::as_ref().into(), how already suggest for @Boqun Feng

I'm a started in the linux kernel contributor, and I can sent a patch for this... My change is anything like 

diff --git a/rust/kernel/device.rs b/rust/kernel/device.rs
index 851018eef885..915abc9e7616 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/device.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/device.rs
@@ -41,28 +41,15 @@
 pub struct Device(Opaque<bindings::device>);

 impl Device {
-    /// Creates a new reference-counted abstraction instance of an existing `struct device` pointer.
+    /// Gets a refcounted reference to [`Self`]
     ///
     /// # Safety
-    ///
-    /// Callers must ensure that `ptr` is valid, non-null, and has a non-zero reference count,
-    /// i.e. it must be ensured that the reference count of the C `struct device` `ptr` points to
-    /// can't drop to zero, for the duration of this function call.
-    ///
-    /// It must also be ensured that `bindings::device::release` can be called from any thread.
-    /// While not officially documented, this should be the case for any `struct device`.
-    pub unsafe fn from_raw(ptr: *mut bindings::device) -> ARef<Self> {
-        // SAFETY: By the safety requirements, ptr is valid.
-        // Initially increase the reference count by one to compensate for the final decrement once
-        // this newly created `ARef<Device>` instance is dropped.
-        unsafe { bindings::get_device(ptr) };
-
-        // CAST: `Self` is a `repr(transparent)` wrapper around `bindings::device`.
-        let ptr = ptr.cast::<Self>();
-
-        // SAFETY: `ptr` is valid by the safety requirements of this function. By the above call to
-        // `bindings::get_device` we also own a reference to the underlying `struct device`.
-        unsafe { ARef::from_raw(ptr::NonNull::new_unchecked(ptr)) }
+    /// `ptr` must be a valid pointer with at least one reference count until the function return.
+    pub unsafe fn get_from_raw(ptr: *mut bindings::device) -> ARef<Self> {
+        // SAFETY: Per function safety requirement, `ptr` is a valid pointer and has at least one
+        // reference count until the function return, which is larger then the scope of the `&Device`
+        // created by `Device::as_ref(ptr)`.
+        unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into()
     }

     /// Obtain the raw `struct device *`.
diff --git a/rust/kernel/firmware.rs b/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
index dee5b4b18aec..23a0a758fc72 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
@@ -44,7 +44,7 @@ fn request_nowarn() -> Self {
 ///
 /// # fn no_run() -> Result<(), Error> {
 /// # // SAFETY: *NOT* safe, just for the example to get an `ARef<Device>` instance
-/// # let dev = unsafe { Device::from_raw(core::ptr::null_mut()) };
+/// # let dev = unsafe { Device::get_from_raw(core::ptr::null_mut()) };
 ///
 /// let fw = Firmware::request(c_str!("path/to/firmware.bin"), &dev)?;
 /// let blob = fw.data();

In the truth, I'm confused, because in the example showing by @Boqun Feng , he removed the line that increment refcount. But if function don't increment refcount, then your name don't need changed, becuase will be equal the Arc::from_raw that don't increment refcount too .. .

I think that the function Device::from_raw() only need change your name for Device::get_from_raw() or Device::get_device(). And I prefer Device::get_device() for be equal de get_device() in .c file.

Then, this change is anything like this: 

diff --git a/rust/kernel/device.rs b/rust/kernel/device.rs
index 851018eef885..5dfbe7c0824c 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/device.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/device.rs
@@ -51,7 +51,7 @@ impl Device {
     ///
     /// It must also be ensured that `bindings::device::release` can be called from any thread.
     /// While not officially documented, this should be the case for any `struct device`.
-    pub unsafe fn from_raw(ptr: *mut bindings::device) -> ARef<Self> {
+    pub unsafe fn get_from_raw(ptr: *mut bindings::device) -> ARef<Self> {
         // SAFETY: By the safety requirements, ptr is valid.
         // Initially increase the reference count by one to compensate for the final decrement once
         // this newly created `ARef<Device>` instance is dropped.
diff --git a/rust/kernel/firmware.rs b/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
index dee5b4b18aec..23a0a758fc72 100644
--- a/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
+++ b/rust/kernel/firmware.rs
@@ -44,7 +44,7 @@ fn request_nowarn() -> Self {
 ///
 /// # fn no_run() -> Result<(), Error> {
 /// # // SAFETY: *NOT* safe, just for the example to get an `ARef<Device>` instance
-/// # let dev = unsafe { Device::from_raw(core::ptr::null_mut()) };
+/// # let dev = unsafe { Device::get_from_raw(core::ptr::null_mut()) };
 ///
 /// let fw = Firmware::request(c_str!("path/to/firmware.bin"), &dev)?;
 /// let blob = fw.data();

For this way, your implementation will be equal before. But your name is differente so as not to confuse with Arc::from_raw().

Panagiotis Foliadis said:

Sorry for the late response!
I have a question about the examples that I need to add to the documentation.
For the case of the Device::as_ref I believe the 

let dev = unsafe { Device::as_ref(ptr) }.into();

is enough, however im struggling to find a correct example to add to the ARef struct that connects to the removal of the Device::from_raw function (since the github issues specifies that an example should be added to the ARef struct).
The Device::as_ref + into() doesnt have anything to do with the ARef struct right?

Note that in my example, into()’s return type is ARef<Device>
